content.full_text:
Memorandu

To : OPINION REQUEST DIVISION LA [illegible]

April 14, 1978

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
John Simsello

Subject:

A statewide miscellaneous committee for appellant political committee
contributes $300 to a candidate for city council in a local jurisdiction.
The contribution is filed on January 23 and the candidate
filing for the local office closes January 23 and 24 the
miscellaneous committee files an amended
statement but does not file it final. On February 1 the
committee files late contribution report for an additional
a late contribution must report it within ten days with what
committee is required to file a late contribution statement for the election
(see Section 84211).

On March 1 the committee receives notification of
$1,000 which it intends to be used, in connection with the ad-side primary.
None of the $1,000 will be spent in the local election and the
miscellaneous committee has no intention to make further contribution
to the local candidate. Must the miscellaneous committee file a late
contribution report in the local jurisdiction?

PROPOSED:

1. Recipient:

Committee that receives $1,000 must file a late contribution
report and report receipt of contribution on post election
statement.

84214 does not distinguish among reasons that contributions are
received (no filing obligation if committee had declared the
second abbreviated statement to be "final").

2. Donor:

a. If donor is not a committee, no filing obligations at all -
84214 applies only to "candidates or committees".

b. If donor is a committee - still no filing obligation
(unless the committee has made other contributions in this
election account - 84214 applies only to committees "required
to file a campaign statement for the election," and this
committee has no filing obligations in this election.

In summary:

The recipient must file but the donor is not required to do
so.

sections.question: A statewide miscellaneous committee (recipient committee) contributes $300 to a candidate for city council in a local jurisdiction. The contribution is made January 22. The first pre-election filing for the local election closes January 23 and is the January 26. The miscellaneous committee files an abbreviated statement but does not declare it final. On February 23 the committee files another abbreviated statement for the second pre-election period, which closed February 26. From February 21 to March 7, the date of the local election, each committee which receives a late contribution must report it to each office with which that committee is required to file a campaign statement for the election (see Section 84214). On March 1 the miscellaneous committee receives a contribution of $1,000 which it intends to use, and when the donor intends to be used, in connection with the upcoming June statewide primary. None of the $1,000 will be spent in the local election and the miscellaneous committee has no intention to make further contributions to the local candidate. Must the miscellaneous committee file a late contribution report in the local jurisdiction?
sections.conclusion: The recipient must file but the donor is not required to do so. The committee that receives $1,000 must file a late contribution report and report receipt of contribution on post election statement. Section 84214 does not distinguish among reasons that contributions are received. If the donor is not a committee, no filing obligations exist at all. If the donor is a committee, still no filing obligation applies unless the committee has made other contributions in this election, as 84214 applies only to committees 'required to file a campaign statement for the election.'
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a statewide miscellaneous committee that receives a $1,000 contribution on March 1 (intended for a June statewide primary, not a local election on March 7) must file a late contribution report in the local jurisdiction where it previously made a $300 contribution to a city council candidate.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The recipient committee must file a late contribution report for the $1,000 received on March 1, as Section 84214 does not distinguish based on the intended use of funds. However, the donor has no filing obligation in the local jurisdiction unless it is itself a committee with other filing obligations in that election.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum format advice letter from FPPC. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers, but question and response sections are clearly delineated. The document references Section 84214 throughout. Confidence slightly reduced due to formatting variations from standard advice letter format.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A statewide miscellaneous committee (recipient committee) contributes $300 to a candidate for city council in a local jurisdiction. The contribution is made January 22. The first pre-election filing for the local election closes January 23 and is the January 26. The miscellaneous committee files an abbreviated statement but does not declare it final. On February 23 the committee files another abbreviated statement for the second pre-election period, which closed February 26. From February 21 to March 7, the date of the local election, each committee which receives a late contribution must report it to each office with which that committee is required to file a campaign statement for the election (see Section 84214). On March 1 the miscellaneous committee receives a contribution of $1,000 which it intends to use, and when the donor intends to be used, in connection with the upcoming June statewide primary. None of the $1,000 will be spent in the local election and the miscellaneous committee has no intention to make further contributions to the local candidate. Must the miscellaneous committee file a late contribution report in the local jurisdiction?

CONCLUSION: The recipient must file but the donor is not required to do so. The committee that receives $1,000 must file a late contribution report and report receipt of contribution on post election statement. Section 84214 does not distinguish among reasons that contributions are received. If the donor is not a committee, no filing obligations exist at all. If the donor is a committee, still no filing obligation applies unless the committee has made other contributions in this election, as 84214 applies only to committees 'required to file a campaign statement for the election.'

The contribution is filed on January 23 and the candidate filing for the local office closes January 23 and 24 the miscellaneous committee files an amended statement but does not file it final. On February 1 the committee files late contribution report for an additional a late contribution must report it within ten days with what committee is required to file a late contribution statement for the election (see Section 84211). On March 1 the committee receives notification of $1,000 which it intends to be used, in connection with the ad-side primary. Must the miscellaneous committee file a late contribution report in the local jurisdiction? PROPOSED: 1. Recipient: Committee that receives $1,000 must file a late contribution report and report receipt of contribution on post election statement. 84214 does not distinguish among reasons that contributions are received (no filing obligation if committee had declared the second abbreviated statement to be "final"). 2. Donor: a. If donor is not a committee, no filing obligations at all - 84214 applies only to "candidates or committees". b. If donor is a committee - still no filing obligation (unless the committee has made other contributions in this election account - 84214 applies only to committees "required to file a campaign statement for the election," and this committee has no filing obligations in this election. embedding.summary: FPPC advises on whether a miscellaneous committee must file a late contribution report in a local jurisdiction for a $1,000 contribution received March 1 that was intended for a June statewide primary, not the local March 7 election. The recipient committee must file, but the donor has no filing obligation.
